  • Analyze Gender Ratios: Why did 19th-century migration lead to a dramatic shift in gender responsibilities inside home countries?

  • Define the Purpose of Enclaves: Understand that ethnic enclaves acted as a cultural buffer, helping immigrants survive harsh or unwelcoming environments.

  • Identify Legal Backlash: Be ready to list and analyze the specific national laws used to curb Asian immigration during the peak of global globalization.

docs.title: AP World History Unit 6 Comprehensive Review: Consequences of Industrialization (1750–1900)
1. The Core Motives: Rationales for Imperialism
docs.text:
The Industrial Revolution created an enormous shift in global power dynamics. Wealthy industrial nations developed core ideological, cultural, and political rationales to justify their aggressive territorial expansion.

  • The Core Drivers:

    • Cultural and Racial Ideologies: Concepts like Social Darwinism distorted biological evolutionary theory to argue that white Europeans were racially superior and destined to conquer "less fit" societies.

    • The "Civilizing Mission": Western powers asserted an obligation to spread features of modern civilization—including technology, industrial infrastructure, medicine, and Western law—to non-industrialized populations.

    • Religious Conversion: Christian missionaries traveled extensively to set up schools and churches, aiming to religiously convert indigenous populations.

    • Nationalism: Acquiring overseas colonies became a prime competitive status symbol for European states, the United States, and Meiji Japan to project global power.

docs.title: 2. The Methods: State Expansion and Imperial Shifts
docs.text:
Imperialism shifted away from the early modern model of loose, coastal trading posts toward direct territorial conquest, political absorption, and settler colonization.

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        SHIFTS IN COLONIAL GOVERNANCE                   │
└───────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    │
       ┌────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────┐
       ▼                                                         ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│   NON-STATE / CORPORATE RULE    │ ───>  │       DIRECT STATE CONTROL   │

│ Managed by joint-stock ventures │       │ Governed directly by imperial│

│ for purely mercantile profit.   │     │metropoles and military forces. │
└─────────────────────────────────┘    └─────────────────────────────────┘

  • The Institutional Shifts:

    • The Belgian Congo: King Leopold II originally ran the Congo Basin as a brutal, private corporate monopoly. Following global outcry over rubber harvesting atrocities, the Belgian government stepped in and transitioned it into a direct state colony.

    • British India: Control shifted cleanly from the private corporate military force of the British East India Company to direct British Crown governance (the British Raj) following the widespread Indian Rebellion of 1857.

    • The Dutch East Indies: The bankrupt Dutch East India Company (VOC) was formally dissolved, transferring direct political control of Indonesia over to the official Dutch government.

  • New Forms of Expansion:

    • The Scramble for Africa: European powers used diplomacy (the Berlin Conference) and industrial warfare to slice up 90% of the African continent into states.

    • Settler Colonies: Empires established settler colonies—such as the British in New Zealand—where large waves of white European families permanently relocated to replace or dominate the indigenous population.

    • Regional Conquests: The United States, Russia, and Meiji Japan rapidly expanded their immediate land holdings by conquering and settling neighboring indigenous territories.

docs.title: 3. The Resistance: Indigenous Pushback
docs.text: Indigenous populations across the globe actively countered imperial expansion through direct armed conflicts, political organizing, and supernatural or religious resistance movements.

docs.title: Major Anti-Colonial Direct Resistance
docs.text:
• The 1857 Rebellion in India: Sepoys revolted over religious insults to Hindu and Muslim dietary traditions, triggering a massive regional anti-colonial war that forced the British state to take direct control of governance.

  • The Yaa Asantewaa War: In West Africa, Queen Mother Yaa Asantewaa led the Asante Kingdom in a fierce military defense to protect their sacred Golden Stool from British imperial capture.

  • The Balkan Independent States: Diverse ethnic populations in the Balkan peninsula organized national resistance movements to break away from the declining land-based Ottoman Empire, forming new, independent states.

docs.title: Religious and Spiritual Resistance
docs.text:
When confronted with superior Western industrial weapons, several indigenous societies turned to spiritual movements to protect their sovereignty:

  • The Ghost Dance (USA): Native American Plains nations united in a sacred dance ritual believed to make white settlers disappear and bring back their ancestral lands.

  • The Xhosa Cattle-Killing Movement (South Africa): Faced with British expansion and livestock disease, the Xhosa mass-slaughtered their own cattle based on a prophecy that ancestral spirits would arise to sweep the British into the ocean.

  • The Mahdist Wars (Sudan): An Islamic cleric declared himself the Mahdi and led a fundamentalist holy war to purge British and Egyptian forces from East Africa.

docs.title: 4. The Economics: Resource Extraction and Imperial Traps
docs.text: The global economic structure was fundamentally organized to serve Western industrial manufacturing advantage, trapping weaker nations into highly exploitative relationships.

docs.title: Export Economies vs. Economic Imperialism
docs.text:
• Export Economies (Raw Resource Extraction): Industrialized cities required massive agricultural and raw inputs. Non-industrialized regions were converted into specialized resource exporters, abandoning self-sufficient farming to harvest single commodities.

    • Cotton: Mass-cultivated in Egypt and South Asia to feed European textile mills.

    • Rubber and Palm Oil: Extracted from the Congo basin, Amazon, and West Africa to act as tires, machine belts, and vital industrial lubricants.

    • Guano: Mined in Peru and Chile to serve as fertilizer for high-yield farming.

    • Meat: Exported from Argentina via new commercial refrigerated steamships.

  • Economic Imperialism (Financial Domination): Rather than claiming land, Western countries used corporate monopolies, debt leverage, and forced trade treaties to subvert sovereign foreign markets.

    • The Opium Wars: Britain and France smuggled South Asian opium into China to intentionally correct a trade deficit. They used naval victories to force open Chinese markets and carve out exclusionary economic Spheres of Influence.

    • British Firms in Argentina: British capitalists fully financed and controlled the modernized Port of Buenos Aires and local rail systems, gaining total economic advantage over Argentina's export profits without ever establishing a formal political colony.

docs.title: 5. The Demographics: Causes and Effects of Global Migration
docs.text: Industrialization and imperial networks created an era of unprecedented global migration, shifting demographics and sparking deep social and political changes around the world.

docs.title: Causes of Migration (Push/Pull Dynamics)
docs.text:
• Push Factors: Extreme population pressures, land shortages, poverty, and local resource destruction (e.g., the Great Famine in Ireland or colonial displacement in India).

  • Pull Factors: Rapid global urbanization and industrial factory jobs drew millions of laborers to major cities.

  • The Tech Catalyst: New transportation systems—such as commercial steamships and cross-continental railroads—made seasonal and long-distance intercontinental relocation highly accessible and affordable.

docs.title: Effects of Migration
docs.text:
• Social Impact on Home Countries: Because physical labor demands meant that migrants were predominantly male, women left behind took on dynamic new roles. They assumed complete responsibility for local family finances, agricultural fields, and domestic businesses formerly managed by men.

  • Cultural Transmission (Ethnic Enclaves): Migrants settled closely together in receiving nations to preserve their heritage, establishing unique ethnic enclaves.

    • Examples: Chinese communities throughout Southeast Asia and the Americas; Indian enclaves across Southern and East Africa; and Italian neighborhoods in Argentina.

  • Political Resistance and Xenophobia: Native populations and receiving governments frequently reacted with deep racial prejudice and economic fear. States passed strict discriminatory laws to purposefully control and block non-white immigration across their borders.

    • Examples: The Chinese Exclusion Act (1882) in the United States and the explicitly restrictive White Australia Policy (1901).

docs.title: 💡 What is Most Important for the Exam?
docs.text:
1. The Continuity of Exploitation: Connect Unit 6 to the Industrial Revolution. Empires didn't conquer just for pride; they conquered because factories had a systemic, insatiable need for raw materials and captive consumer markets.

  2. Supernatural Defiance: Remember that indigenous resistance wasn't just military; movements like the Ghost Dance and Xhosa Cattle-Killing show how deep cultural and spiritual beliefs were used to try and combat superior Western technology.

  3. The Migration Loop: Industrialization created the technology that allowed mass migration (steamships), the jobs that pulled migrants (railroads/plantations), the demographic shifts at home (women taking power), and the legal backlashes abroad (Chinese Exclusion Act).

docs.title: Unit 7.0 (not a Unit) WW1 & WW2 Mega Timeline
1. The Road to World War I (The Fuse)
docs.text:
• 1882 – The Triple Alliance Formed: Germany, Austria-Hungary, and Italy sign a mutual defense pact, splitting Europe into armed camps.

  • 1906 – Launch of HMS Dreadnought: Great Britain launches a revolutionary warship, kicking the Anglo-German naval arms race (Militarism) into overdrive.

  • 1907 – The Triple Entente Formed: Alarmed by Germany's growth, Great Britain, France, and Russia align.

  • June 28, 1914 – The Spark 💥: Serbian nationalist Gavrilo Princip assassinates Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo.

  • July 1914 – The July Crisis: A diplomatic domino effect occurs as alliances activate. Austria-Hungary declares war on Serbia; Russia mobilizes.

docs.title: 2. World War I (1914–1918)
docs.text:
• August 1914 – Outbreak of War: Germany enacts the Schlieffen Plan, invading neutral Belgium to strike France. Great Britain enters the war.

  • Late 1914 – The Race to the Sea: Both sides dig in on the Western Front, establishing a permanent, brutal system of Trench Warfare and strategic stalemate.

  • 1915 – The Armenian Genocide Begins: Under the cover of total war, the Ottoman Empire begins the systematic deportation and massacre of its Christian Armenian minority.

  • 1915 – Industrial Carnage Escalates: First massive use of poison gas at the Second Battle of Ypres. The sinking of the passenger liner Lusitania by German U-boats strains US neutrality.

  • 1916 – The Battles of Attrition:

    • Battle of Verdun: The longest battle of the war, resulting in over 700,000 casualties.

    • Battle of the Somme: The British introduce the tank to break the stalemate; over 1 million men are killed or wounded.

  • March 1917 – The Russian Collapse (Part I): Severe food shortages and economic ruin force Tsar Nicholas II to abdicate during the February Revolution.

  • April 1917 – US Entry: Provoked by unrestricted submarine warfare and the Zimmermann Telegram, the United States declares war on Germany.

  • November 1917 – The Bolshevik Revolution: Vladimir Lenin and the communist Bolsheviks seize power in Russia, promising "Peace, Land, and Bread."

  • March 1918 – Treaty of Brest-Litovsk: Soviet Russia officially exits WWI, ceding massive amounts of territory to Germany.

  • November 11, 1918 – Armistice Day 🕊️: Germany, facing economic collapse and imminent military defeat, signs an armistice. The guns fall silent at 11:00 AM.

docs.title: 3. The Interwar Period (1919–1938)
docs.text:
• June 1919 – Treaty of Versailles: The Allies impose harsh peace terms on Germany (War Guilt Clause, crippling reparations). The League of Nations is created but lacks enforcement power.

  • May 4, 1919 – The May Fourth Movement: Angry Chinese students protest the Versailles decision to award German territory in China to Japan, accelerating Chinese nationalism.

  • 1922 – Rise of Fascism in Italy: Benito Mussolini leads the "March on Rome" and establishes a fascist dictatorship. The Ottoman Empire is officially abolished, replaced by Atatürk's secular Republic of Turkey in 1923.

  • 1923 – German Hyperinflation: Economic collapse forces Germany to overprint currency, rendering the German Mark completely worthless.

  • October 1929 – Black Tuesday 📉: The US Stock Market crashes, triggering the Great Depression and destabilizing global free-market economies.

  • 1928–1932 – Stalin's First Five-Year Plan: Joseph Stalin enforces the collectivization of agriculture in the USSR, leading directly to the man-made Holodomor famine in Ukraine (1932–1933).

  • 1931 – Invasion of Manchuria: Imperial Japan invades northern China for natural resources. The League of Nations objects but does nothing.

  • 1933 – Hitler Seizes Power: Adolf Hitler is appointed Chancellor of Germany, quickly dismantling democracy to establish the totalitarian Third Reich.

  • 1935 – Invasion of Ethiopia: Fascist Italy invades Ethiopia. The League of Nations fails to intervene effectively.

  • 1936 – Remilitarization of the Rhineland: Hitler openly defies the Treaty of Versailles by marching troops into the French border zone. The West watches.

  • September 1938 – The Munich Conference (Appeasement): Britain and France agree to let Hitler annex the Sudetenland (Czechoslovakia) in exchange for a promise of no future expansion.

docs.title: 4. World War II (1939–1945)
docs.text:
• August 1939 – The Nazi-Soviet Pact: Shocking alliance between Hitler and Stalin; secretly plans the partition of Poland.

  • September 1, 1939 – The Invasion of Poland 🚀: Germany unleashes Blitzkrieg tactics on Poland. Britain and France declare war. WWII begins.

  • June 1940 – The Fall of France: Nazi forces rapidly bypass the Maginot Line, conquering France in weeks.

  • Late 1940 – The Battle of Britain: The Royal Air Force successfully holds off a relentless German bombing campaign, preventing a Nazi invasion of Great Britain.

  • June 1941 – Operation Barbarossa: Hitler betrays Stalin and launches a massive invasion of the Soviet Union, opening the brutal Eastern Front.

  • December 7, 1941 – Pearl Harbor: Japan launches a surprise attack on the US naval base in Hawaii. The United States officially enters World War II.

  • 1941–1942 – The Holocaust Escalates: The Nazi regime transitions from institutionalized discrimination to the "Final Solution," building industrial extermination camps like Auschwitz-Birkenau.

  • June 1942 – Battle of Midway 🌊: The US Navy intercepts Japanese codes and sinks four Japanese aircraft carriers, marking the turning point of the war in the Pacific.

  • Feb 1943 – Battle of Stalingrad: The Soviet Red Army forces the surrender of the German 6th Army. This catastrophic defeat marks the turning point of the war in Europe; Germany begins a steady retreat.

  • 1943 – Island Hopping Strategy Begins: US forces selectively target and seize strategic islands across the Pacific to establish forward airfields.

  • June 6, 1944 – D-Day (The Normandy Landings): Allied forces launch the largest amphibious invasion in history, opening up the Western Front in France.

  • May 8, 1945 – V-E Day 🎉: Following the capture of Berlin by Soviet forces and Hitler's suicide, Germany unconditionally surrenders.

  • August 6 & 9, 1945 – The Atomic Bombs: The US drops atomic weapons on Hiroshima and Nagasaki to force a rapid surrender and avoid a land invasion.

  • September 2, 1945 – V-J Day: Japan formally surrenders aboard the USS Missouri, bringing World War II to an official close.

docs.title: 5. The Immediate Aftermath & The Shift
docs.text:
• October 1945 – Founding of the United Nations: Replaces the failed League of Nations, featuring a Security Council with enforcement powers to maintain international stability.

  • 1946–1947 – The Iron Curtain Descends: The temporary alliance between the democratic Western Allies and the communist Soviet Union fractures, instantly creating the geopolitical landscape of the Cold War.

docs.title: 1. The Big Picture: Empires in Collapse
docs.text:
As the 20th century dawned, the old-school land-based empires that had dominated global politics for centuries began to crack under the weight of internal economic stagnation, ethnic tensions, and a failure to modernize. By the 1920s, four major empires collapsed entirely, reshuffling the global balance of power.

                  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
                  │  Land-Based Empires (Pre-1900)       │
                  └──────────────────┬──────────────────┘
                                     │
         ┌───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┐
         ▼                           ▼                           ▼
┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐
│   Ottoman       │         │   Qing Dynasty  │         │   Russian       │
│  "Sick Man"     │         │  Foreign Rule   │         │  Romanov Fall   │
└────────┬────────┘         └────────┬────────┘         └────────┬────────┘
         ▼                           ▼                           ▼
┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐
│ Young Turks /   │         │ Sun Yat-sen /   │         │ Lenin / Soviet  │
│ Republic of TR  │         │ Republic of CN  │         │ Union (USSR)    │
└─────────────────┘         └─────────────────┘         └─────────────────┘

docs.title: A. The Russian Empire (1917)
docs.text:
• The Root Causes: Russia was slow to industrialize, leaving its massive peasant population impoverished. Political power was heavily centralized under the absolute rule of Tsar Nicholas II (Romanov Dynasty).

  • The Spark: The devastation and food shortages of World War I pushed the population over the edge.

  • The Result: The Bolshevik Revolution (1917), led by Vladimir Lenin.

    • The Romanov dynasty was overthrown and executed.

    • The Bolsheviks established the world's first communist state, transforming Russia into the Soviet Union (USSR).

    • Economic Shift: The government seized control of land, factories, and resources, replacing a weak capitalist economy with a state-directed command economy.

docs.title: B. The Qing Dynasty of China (1911)
docs.text:
• The Root Causes: Deep internal ethnic tension between the ruling Manchus (a minority) and the Han Chinese majority. Constant foreign intervention (via unequal treaties and Spheres of Influence) weakened the dynasty's legitimacy.

  • The Revolution: The Xinhai Revolution (1911), led by Sun Yat-sen.

  • The Ideology: Sun Yat-sen championed the Three Principles of the People:

    1. Nationalism: Overthrowing Manchu rule and ending foreign imperialist intervention.

    2. Democracy: Establishing a constitutional republic.

    3. Livelihood: Ensuring economic security and land equalization for the peasantry.

  • The Sequel: The fall of the Qing did not lead to immediate stability. Instead, China fractured into a chaotic Warlord Era, setting up a long internal conflict between the Chinese Nationalist Party (Kuomintang) and the rising Chinese Communist Party (CCP).

docs.title: C. The Ottoman Empire (1922)
docs.text:
• The Root Causes: Known as the "Sick Man of Europe," the empire suffered from severe economic debt to Western banks and a failure to effectively industrialize. Heavy pushback from diverse ethnic minorities seeking independence (especially in the Balkans) tore the empire apart.

  • The Spark: The Ottomans sided with the Central Powers (Germany and Austria-Hungary) in WWI and lost. The empire was partitioned by European powers via the Treaty of Sèvres.

  • The Rebirth: Turkish nationalists, led by Mustafa Kemal (Atatürk), fought a war of independence against foreign occupiers.

    • They abolished the Ottoman Sultanate and established the secular Republic of Turkey in 1923.

    • Atatürk launched radical modernization and westernization reforms: switching to the Latin alphabet, granting women legal rights, and stripping Islamic institutions of political authority.

docs.title: D. The Mexican Revolution (1910–1920)
docs.text:
• The Root Causes: Mexico was ruled by the authoritarian dictator Porfirio Díaz, whose policies heavily favored wealthy elites and foreign investors. Nearly 95% of rural peasants owned no land at all.

  • The Conflict: A brutal, decade-long civil war broke out involving various revolutionary factions led by figures like Francisco Madero, Emiliano Zapata (who demanded Tierra y Libertad—Land and Liberty), and Pancho Villa.

  • The Resolution: The adoption of the Mexican Constitution of 1917, which set a template for modern social reform:

    • Land Reform: Redistribution of land back to communal peasant groups (ejidos).

    • Labor Rights: Universal suffrage, minimum wage laws, 8-hour workdays, and the right to strike.

    • Resource Control: Stripped foreign corporations of natural resource ownership, stating that all subsoil rights belong to the Mexican nation.

docs.title: AP World History Unit 7.2: Causes of World War I
1. The Underlying Causes: M-A-I-N
docs.text:
World War I didn’t just happen out of nowhere. Decades of tension built up a global powder keg. The easiest way to remember the long-term causes is the classic acronym MAIN:

 ───  M  ───►  MILITARISM      ───►  Massive industrial arms race
 ───  A  ───►  ALLIANCES       ───►  Secret treaties lock nations together
 ───  I  ───►  IMPERIALISM     ───►  Fierce global competition for colonies
 ───  N  ───►  NATIONALISM     ───►  Extreme pride & ethnic desire for independence

docs.title: Militarism
docs.text:
• What it was: An aggressive glorification of military might and a massive buildup of weapons.

  • The Dynamic: Driven by the Industrial Revolution, European powers competed to see who could amass the biggest, baddest military.

  • The Anglo-German Naval Race: Germany, trying to establish itself as a dominant world power, started rapidly building up its navy. This directly threatened Great Britain, the traditional ruler of the seas. Both sides poured massive capital into building massive, heavily armored warships called Dreadnoughts.

docs.title: Alliances
docs.text:
• What it was: Treaties binding countries to defend one another if attacked. Intended to act as a deterrent to war, they instead acted as a tripwire.

  • The Two Blocs:

    • Triple Entente: Great Britain, France, and Russia.

    • Triple Alliance: Germany, Austria-Hungary, and Italy (though Italy later flipped sides).

docs.title: Imperialism
docs.text:
• What it was: The scramble for overseas colonies, market control, and raw materials.

  • The Dynamic: European powers had spent the late 19th century carving up Africa and Asia (think Berlin Conference). By 1914, there was almost no land left to conquer, so empires began clashing over existing boundaries, driving mutual suspicion to an all-time high.

docs.title: Nationalism
docs.text:
• What it was: Intense pride in one's country or ethnic group, often coupled with the belief that your group is superior to others.

  • The Balkans: Known as the "Powder Keg of Europe." This region was a volatile mix of diverse ethnic groups. The Austro-Hungarian Empire controlled much of this territory, but Slavic nationalist groups (especially Serbians) fiercely wanted independence to form a unified Slavic state.

docs.title: 2. The Spark: The Assassination of Franz Ferdinand
docs.text:
While MAIN laid out the dry kindling, a single assassination lit the match.

  • The Target: Archduke Franz Ferdinand, heir to the Austro-Hungarian throne.

  • The Action: On June 28, 1914, while visiting Sarajevo, Bosnia, the Archduke and his wife were shot and killed.

  • The Assassin: Gavrilo Princip, a 19-year-old Serbian nationalist and member of a radical group called the Black Hand.

docs.title: The July Crisis Domino Effect
docs.text:
1. Austria-Hungary blames Serbia for the assassination and issues a harsh ultimatum.

  2. Serbia refuses to comply fully.

  3. Austria-Hungary declares war on Serbia.

  4. Russia (sharing Slavic ethnic ties) mobilizes its military to defend Serbia.

  5. Germany views Russian mobilization as an act of war and declares war on Russia and its ally, France.

  6. Great Britain joins the fight when Germany marches through neutral Belgium to invade France.

docs.title: 1. The New Era of Warfare: Total War
docs.text:
World War I was not just a battle between armies; it was a clash between entire societies. This conflict marked the dawn of Total War.

  • What it means: A conflict in which participating countries devote all their resources—human, industrial, and financial—to the war effort.

  • The Home Front: Governments took control of domestic economies to prioritize military production.

    • Rationing: Citizens faced strict limits on everyday goods like sugar, meat, fuel, and rubber so supplies could go to the front lines.

    • The Draft (Conscription): Millions of young men were legally required to join the military.

    • Women in the Workforce: With millions of men at war, women stepped up to work in ammunition factories, drive transit, and run farms. This shift fundamentally altered the social perception of women's capabilities.

                  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
                  │          THE TOTAL WAR MATRIX          │
                  └──────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                                     │
         ┌───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┐
         ▼                           ▼                           ▼
┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐        ┌─────────────────┐
│   The Economy   │         │ The Human Cost  │         │   The Mind     │

│ Rationing, factory│       │ Draft/conscription│       │ Propaganda, war│

│ retooling for ammo│       │ women in factories│       │media censorship│
└─────────────────┘         └─────────────────┘        └─────────────────┘

docs.title: 2. Shaping the Narrative: Propaganda & Censorship
docs.text:
To keep populations committed to a brutal, drawn-out war, governments had to control information.

  • Propaganda: Media (posters, articles, films) designed to influence public opinion. It highly emotionalized the conflict by demonizing the enemy, exaggerating atrocities, and appealing to national pride to boost enlistment and war bond purchases.

  • Censorship: Governments heavily restricted the press. Negative news, true casualty counts, or letters from soldiers complaining about the horrors of the trenches were blacked out to prevent the collapse of domestic morale.

docs.title: 3. Industrial Carnage: New Military Technologies
docs.text:
The deadly combination of Industrial Revolution manufacturing and traditional military tactics resulted in unprecedented slaughter. Defense completely outmatched offense.

  • Trench Warfare: To survive new weapons, armies dug massive networks of protective ditches stretching across the Western Front (France/Germany border). This created a permanent stalemate where neither side could advance without catastrophic casualties.

  • The Tech Upgrades:

    • Machine Guns: Fired hundreds of rounds per minute, making old-school infantry charges across open fields suicidal.

    • Poison Gas: Chlorine and mustard gas blinded, choked, and suffocated soldiers. It introduced chemical warfare to the modern era, forcing the invention of gas masks.

    • Tanks: Armored vehicles developed by the British to roll over barbed wire and traverse muddy trenches, breaking the tactical stalemate.

    • Submarines (U-boats): Extensively used by Germany to sink allied cargo and military ships, choking off enemy supply lines.

docs.title: 4. A Truly Global Conflict
docs.text: World War I was not confined to Europe; imperial networks dragged the entire globe into the trenches.

docs.title: A. Colonial Troops
docs.text:
European empires did not have enough domestic manpower, so they pulled millions of soldiers and laborers from their overseas colonies.

  • The Promise: Many colonial subjects fought willingly, believing that their loyalty and sacrifice would earn them independence or citizenship after the war (Spoiler: It didn't).

  • Major Contributions:

    • India: Sent over 1 million troops to fight for Great Britain in Europe and the Middle East.

    • West Africa & Indochina: Hundreds of thousands of soldiers fought directly under the French flag.

    • ANZAC: Australian and New Zealand Army Corps played a massive role in campaigns like Gallipoli.

docs.title: B. Global Theaters of War
docs.text:
• The Middle East: The British allied with Arab nationalists to launch the Arab Revolt against the Ottoman Empire, successfully destabilizing Ottoman control from within.

  • East Asia & Africa: Japan (allied with Britain) seized German colonial outposts in China and the Pacific, while Allied forces invaded German colonies in Africa.

docs.title: Unit 7.4   Economy in the Interwar Period
1. The Global Economy After WWI:
docs.text: The roaring twenties didn't roar everywhere, and they definitely didn't last. The massive financial cost of World War I, combined with poor economic planning, threw the world into the Great Depression by 1929.

docs.title: Germany's Hyperinflation
docs.text:
• The Problem: The Treaty of Versailles forced Germany to pay massive war reparations. Germany didn't have the money, so the government simply printed more.

  • The Result: Hyperinflation. The German Mark became completely worthless. People literally used stacks of cash as building blocks for kids or burned money to keep their houses warm because it was cheaper than buying wood.

docs.title: 📉 The U.S. Stock Market Crash (1929)
docs.text:
• The Spark: On "Black Tuesday" (October 29, 1929), the U.S. stock market collapsed due to over-speculation and buying stocks on margin (borrowed money).

  • The Ripple Effect: Because the U.S. had been lending money to European nations to rebuild, the American crash pulled the rest of the global economy down with it. Global trade plummeted by over 60%.

docs.title: 2. Competing Economic Fixes
docs.text: Nations realized laissez-faire (hands-off) capitalism wasn't fixing the crisis. Governments had to step in. Three radically different economic models emerged:

docs.title: Model A: The New Deal (Regulated Capitalism)
docs.text:
• Who: U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt (FDR).

  • The Strategy: Inspired by Keynesian Economics, which argues that during a recession, the government must spend money to jumpstart the economy.

  • The Actions:

    • Created massive public works projects (building dams, roads, and bridges) to employ millions.

    • Introduced safety nets like Social Security and FDIC banking regulations to prevent future crashes.

docs.title: ☭ Model B: The Five-Year Plans (State Command Economy)
docs.text:
• Who: Joseph Stalin in the Soviet Union (USSR).

  • The Strategy: Total government control over production. Stalin completely rejected capitalism.

  • The Actions:

    • Collectivization of Agriculture: The state seized private farms to create massive, government-run mega-farms. Peasants who resisted (like the Kulaks) were executed or sent to gulags. This chaos directly triggered catastrophic artificial famines, like the Holodomor in Ukraine.

    • Heavy Industrialization: Total focus on factories, steel production, and mining. While brutal, it transformed the USSR from a backwards agrarian society into an industrial powerhouse in record time.

docs.title: Model C: Fascist Corporate Economies
docs.text:
• Who: Benito Mussolini (Italy) and Adolf Hitler (Germany).

  • The Strategy: A hybrid approach. Private property and corporations were allowed to exist, but they had to serve the goals of the fascist state.

  • The Actions: Extreme militarism. Both regimes poured massive amounts of state money into illegal rearmament (building tanks, planes, and weapons) and public infrastructure (like the German Autobahn) to artificially eliminate unemployment.

docs.title: Review
docs.text: Economic System Main Example Government's Role Impact on the People The New Deal United States Regulator & Employer (Intervened to create jobs and protect banks) Restored hope, provided safety nets, preserved democracy. Five-Year Plans Soviet Union Absolute Dictator (Owned all land, factories, and resources) High industrial output, but caused mass famines and loss of freedom. Fascist Economics Germany / Italy Partner & Taskmaster (Allicanced with corporations to militarize) Fixed unemployment via the war machine; stripped labor rights.

docs.title: 1. The Post-WWI Scene
docs.text:
WW1 didn't end all wars, but paused them. Allies kept talking about global peace, but during the writing of treaties, they chose imperialism over stability.

       ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
       │   THE POST-WWI RECIPE FOR UNRESOLVED GLOBAL TENSION   │
       └───────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘
                                   │
         ┌─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┐
         ▼                         ▼                         ▼
┌──────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐
│  The Middle East │     │    East Asia     │     │ Anti-Colonialism │
│ Mandate System   │     │ Japan gets May   │     │ Local autonomy   │
│ (Secret Deals)   │     │ 4th Movement     │     │ desires ignored  │
└──────────────────┘     └──────────────────┘     └──────────────────┘

docs.title: 2. The Middle East & The Mandate System
docs.text:
During the war, the Allies promised Arab leaders independence if they revolted against the Ottoman Empire. Instead, the Allies made a secret pact behind their backs—the Sykes-Picot Agreement—to divide the territory among themselves.

  • The League of Nations Mandates: Rather than granting independence, the Allies created "Mandates." They claimed these territories in the Middle East and Africa weren't "ready" to govern themselves, so France and Britain had to manage them.

  • The Reality: It was just colonialism with a new, bureaucratic name.

    • France got: Syria and Lebanon.

    • Britain got: Palestine, Transjordan, and Iraq.

  • The Fallout: This betrayal planted the seeds for decades of conflict, border disputes, and deep-seated anti-Western sentiment in the region.

docs.title: 3. The Betrayal of East Asia: The May Fourth Movement
docs.text:
China fought on the side of the Allies in WWI, sending over 140,000 laborers to the Western Front. Their main goal? To win back the Chinese territories (Shandong Peninsula) that Germany had seized.

  • The Treaty of Versailles Decision: Instead of returning the land to China, the Allies handed the Shandong Peninsula directly over to Japan.

  • The Spark: On May 4, 1919, thousands of angry Chinese students took to the streets of Beijing to protest the Western betrayal and their own government's weakness.

  • The Importance: The May Fourth Movement was a massive turning point. It catalyzed Chinese nationalism, caused intellectuals to reject Western-style democracy, and directly led many (including a young Mao Zedong) to turn toward Soviet Marxism, setting up the birth of the Chinese Communist Party.

docs.title: 4. Anti-Colonial Resistance Across the Globe
docs.text: Woodrow Wilson (US President) championed the idea of "Self-Determination"—the concept that ethnic groups should have the right to choose their own government. Colonial subjects took this literally, but the Allies only applied it to white Europeans.

docs.title: India: The Rise of Nationalism
docs.text:
• The Broken Promise: India sent over 1 million troops to fight for Britain, expecting self-rule in return. Instead, Britain extended wartime emergency measures that restricted civil liberties.

  • The Massacre: In 1919, British troops fired on an unarmed crowd of peaceful protestors in the Amritsar Massacre, killing hundreds.

  • The Shift: This brutality completely shattered Indian trust in the British Empire. It accelerated the push for full independence, paving the way for Mahatma Gandhi to lead massive non-violent civil disobedience campaigns (like the Salt March).

docs.title: Southeast Asia & Africa
docs.text:
• Indochina (Vietnam): A young nationalist named Ho Chi Minh traveled to the Versailles peace conference to plead for Vietnamese independence from France. He was ignored. He eventually turned to Communism to achieve his goals.

  • Pan-Africanism: Intellectuals across Africa and the diaspora organized Pan-African Congresses to demand an end to European colonial rule, organizing local labor strikes and political resistance.

docs.title: 1. The Road to Global Conflict:
docs.text:
World War II did not start in a vacuum. It was the direct consequence of unresolved issues from World War I, economic desperation from the Great Depression, and the aggressive expansion of totalitarian regimes.

       ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
       │               THE PATHWAY TO WORLD WAR II              │
       └───────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘
                                   │
         ┌─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┐
         ▼                         ▼                         ▼
┌──────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐
│  Versailles Failure│   │ Fascist Expansion │    │ Western Policy   │
│ Bitter Germany & │     │ Imperial Japan,  │    │ Appeasement      │
│ dissatisfied Japan│    │ Italy, & Germany │    │ (Weak League)    │
└──────────────────┘     └──────────────────┘     └──────────────────┘

docs.title: 2. The Legacy of Versailles & The League's Failure
docs.text:
The peace treaties of 1919 sowed the seeds for the next war by angering both the losers and some of the winners.

  • German Resentment: The Treaty of Versailles completely humiliated Germany by forcing them to accept total blame for WWI, stripping them of territory, and imposing massive financial reparations. This tanked their economy and allowed Adolf Hitler to rise to power by promising to tear up the treaty.

  • The Failure of the League of Nations: The international organization meant to prevent war proved completely toothless. It lacked an army, and major powers (like the United States) never joined. When aggressive nations broke international law, the League did nothing but issue weak verbal warnings.

docs.title: 3. Totalitarianism & Fascist Expansion
docs.text: During the 1930s, militaristic and fascist dictatorships began aggressively conquering territory to build empires, directly defying the international community.

docs.title: Japan in Asia
docs.text:
• The Goal: Lacking natural resources of its own, Japan looked to conquer East Asia to feed its industrial economy.

  • The Actions: In 1931, Japan staged an explosion on a railroad line (the Mukden Incident) as an excuse to invade Manchuria (Northern China). By 1937, they launched a full-scale invasion of mainland China, committing brutal atrocities like the Rape of Nanjing.

docs.title: Italy in Africa (Reviving Rome)
docs.text:
• The Goal: Fascist dictator Benito Mussolini wanted to restore the glory of the ancient Roman Empire.

  • The Action: In 1935, Italy invaded Ethiopia. Despite the Ethiopian Emperor Haile Selassie pleading with the League of Nations for help, Western powers did practically nothing, allowing Italy to annex the country.

docs.title: Germany in Europe (Lebensraum)
docs.text:
• The Goal: Hitler wanted to unite all German-speaking people and conquer territory in Eastern Europe for Lebensraum ("living space").

  • The Actions: Hitler systematically broke the Treaty of Versailles:

    • 1935: Rebuilt the German military.

    • 1936: Remilitarized the Rhineland (the border zone with France).

    • 1938: Annexed Austria (Anschluss) and demanded the Sudetenland (a German-speaking region of Czechoslovakia).

docs.title: 4. The Western Response: Appeasement
docs.text:
Instead of stepping in to stop Hitler, Great Britain and France chose a policy of Appeasement—giving in to an aggressor's demands in the hope that they will eventually be satisfied and stop.

  • The Munich Conference (1938): British Prime Minister Neville Chamberlain met with Hitler. To avoid another catastrophic world war, Britain and France agreed to let Hitler take the Sudetenland if he promised not to conquer anything else. Chamberlain famously declared he had secured "peace for our time."

  • The Twist: Hitler completely ignored his promise and swallowed the rest of Czechoslovakia just months later.

docs.title: 5. The Final Countdown to War (1939)
docs.text:
Two shocking diplomatic moves officially triggered the outbreak of World War II:

  1. The Nazi-Soviet Non-Aggression Pact (August 1939): Bitter ideological enemies Adolf Hitler and Joseph Stalin shocked the world by signing a secret agreement promising not to attack each other. Privately, they agreed to divide Poland between themselves. This protected Germany from fighting a two-front war.

  2. The Invasion of Poland (September 1, 1939) : Free from the threat of a Soviet attack, Germany launched a massive invasion of Poland using its lightning-fast Blitzkrieg ("lightning war") strategy.

The Result: Realizing appeasement had failed, Great Britain and France finally drew a line in the sand and declared war on Germany. World War II had begun.

docs.title: 1. Another Total War
docs.text:
Just like its predecessor, World War II was a Total War. Governments mobilized their entire populations, economies, and resources to secure a victory.

  • The Scope: This time, the line between "soldier" and "civilian" was blurred even further. Factories retooled instantly—car manufacturers built tanks, and textile mills spun uniforms.

  • The Political Strategy: Governments leveraged every tool in the shed—propaganda posters, heavily patriotic radio broadcasts, and strict news censorship—to keep civilian morale high and ensure nobody was questioning the massive sacrifice.

docs.title: 2. Fast & Furious: Blitzkrieg vs. Island Hopping
docs.text: The technological stalemate of WWI trenches was completely obliterated by fast, mechanized warfare. Strategy evolved depending on where you were fighting:

docs.title: The European Theater: Blitzkrieg
docs.text:
• The Strategy: Translated as "Lightning War." It relied on a highly coordinated, rapid strike using tanks (panzers), motorized infantry, and overwhelming air support (Luftwaffe).

  • The Goal: Penetrate deep into enemy lines before they even realize what hit them, bypassing traditional defenses.

docs.title: The Pacific Theater: Island Hopping
docs.text:
• The Strategy: Used by the Allied forces (predominantly the US) against the Japanese Empire.

  • The Goal: Instead of wasting lives trying to capture every single island controlled by Japan, the Allies selectively targeted and conquered strategic islands. These captured islands served as stepping-stone airfields, pushing closer and closer to mainland Japan.

       ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
       │                WWII MILITARY STRATEGIES                │
       └───────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘
                                   │
         ┌─────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┐
         ▼                                                   ┌─────────────────────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│     EUROPE: BLITZKRIEG          │       │     PACIFIC: ISLAND HOPPING  │

│  Fast, coordinated land/air     │       │  Strategic island bypasses   │

│  strikes to prevent stalemates. │       │  Moving airfields near Japan.│

└─────────────────────────────────┘    └─────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: 3. The Turning Points
docs.text:
You don't need to know every battle for the AP exam, but you must know the ones that shifted the momentum of the war:

  • The Battle of Britain (1940): A massive air battle where the British Royal Air Force (RAF) successfully defended the UK against relentless German bombing raids. It proved Hitler wasn't invincible.

  • The Battle of Stalingrad (1942–1943): The brutal turning point on the Eastern Front. The Soviet Red Army encircled and destroyed the German 6th Army in a frozen urban wasteland. From this point on, Germany was retreating.

  • The Battle of Midway (1942): The turning point in the Pacific. US naval forces intercepted Japanese code, ambushed their fleet, and sank four irreplaceable Japanese aircraft carriers.

  • D-Day / Normandy Invasion (June 6, 1944): The massive Allied amphibious landing on the beaches of France. It opened up a critical Western Front, forcing Germany to fight a losing battle from both sides.

docs.title: 4. Unprecedented Carnage: The Atomic Bomb
docs.text:
The war in Europe ended in May 1945 (V-E Day), but the war in the Pacific dragged on. Seeking an alternative to a bloody, full-scale land invasion of mainland Japan, US President Harry Truman authorized the use of a terrifying new weapon developed by the Manhattan Project.

  • The Action: The US dropped two nuclear weapons on Japanese cities:

    • Hiroshima (August 6, 1945)

    • Nagasaki (August 9, 1945)

  • The Impact: Over 100,000 civilians were killed instantly, with tens of thousands more dying later from radiation sickness.

  • The Result: Japan formally surrendered on August 15, 1945 (V-J Day), bringing World War II to an official end.

docs.title: What is a Mass Atrocity?
docs.text:
While the 1900s brought rapid technological progress, it also unleashed unprecedented destruction. The rise of extreme nationalism, total war ideologies, and ruthless totalitarian governments led to systematic, state-sponsored violence against specific groups of people.

 ───► STEP 1: EXTREME IDEOLOGY   ───► State-sponsored nationalism or racial supremacy
 ───► STEP 2: CRISIS / WAR       ───► Economic collapse or war provides a smokescreen
 ───► STEP 3: DEHUMANIZATION     ───► Target groups blamed for society's problems
 ───► STEP 4: MASS ATROCITY      ───► Systematic, industrial-scale elimination

docs.title: 2. Key Case Studies of Genocide and Mass Violence
The Armenian Genocide (1915–1917)
docs.text:
• The Context: The Ottoman Empire was collapsing and losing territory during World War I.

  • The Scapegoat: The Christian Armenian minority living within the empire.

  • The Accusation: The Muslim Ottoman government accused the Christian Armenians of conspiring and cooperating with the enemy (the Russian Empire).

  • The Atrocity: Under the cover of WWI, the Ottoman government systematically deported and massacred between 600,000 and 1.5 million Armenians through forced death marches into the Syrian desert, starvation, and execution.

  • The Global Echo: To this day, the modern state of Turkey officially denies that this event constituted a planned "genocide," making it a persistent international political issue.

docs.title: The Holodomor / Ukrainian Famine (1932–1933)
docs.text:
• The Context: Joseph Stalin’s Soviet Union was executing its first Five-Year Plan, which included the total forced collectivization of all agricultural land.

  • The Target: Ukrainian peasants and independent farmers (known as Kulaks) who actively resisted giving up their private land to the communist state.

  • The Atrocity: In response to their resistance, Stalin’s government intentionally set impossible grain requisition quotas and completely sealed Ukraine's borders. The state seized all available food, deliberately creating a man-made, artificial famine.

  • The Toll: Approximately 3.9 to 7 million Ukrainians starved to death in an event now widely recognized as a state-engineered genocide.

docs.title: The Holocaust (1941–1945)
docs.text:
• The Context: Adolf Hitler and the Nazi Party rose to power in Germany by leveraging deep anti-Semitic prejudices and blaming Jewish people for Germany's economic ruin after WWI.

  • The Ideology: Radical Aryan racial supremacy and the pursuit of Lebensraum (living space).

  • The Shift to the "Final Solution": What started as discriminatory laws (the Nuremberg Laws) and forced segregation into crowded ghettos escalated into an industrial-scale plan to completely exterminate Europe's Jewish population.

  • The Factory System of Death: The Nazis built highly organized concentration and extermination camps (like Auschwitz-Birkenau) utilizing specialized technology—such as gas chambers using Zyklon B and high-capacity crematoriums—to maximize efficiency.

  • The Toll: Over 6 million Jews and millions of others deemed "undesirable" by the state (including Roma, people with disabilities, Soviet POWs, and political dissidents) were systematically murdered.

docs.title: The Cambodian Genocide (1975–1979)
docs.text:
• The Context: In the chaotic aftermath of the Vietnam War, a radical communist guerrilla group called the Khmer Rouge, led by the dictator Pol Pot, seized power in Cambodia.

  • The Ideology: Radical agrarian socialism. Pol Pot wanted to completely reset Cambodian society back to "Year Zero" by destroying all modern, Western, urban influence.

  • The Atrocity: The Khmer Rouge emptied the cities, forcing the entire population into rural slave-labor farming communes known as the "Killing Fields." * The Targets: Anyone representing modern society was executed—intellectuals, doctors, teachers, ethnic minorities, and even people who simply wore glasses (as it was seen as a sign of literacy).

  • The Toll: Roughly 1.7 to 2 million people (a quarter of Cambodia’s entire population) died from executions, overwork, and starvation.

docs.title: The Rwandan Genocide (1994)
docs.text:
• The Context: Decades of deep-seated ethnic tension between the Hutu majority and the Tutsi minority, a rivalry that had been intentionally aggravated and weaponized by Belgian colonial rulers years prior.

  • The Catalyst: In April 1994, a plane carrying the Hutu president of Rwanda was shot down. Hutu extremists immediately blamed the Tutsis.

  • The Atrocity: Using state-controlled radio stations to spread hateful propaganda (referring to Tutsis as "cockroaches"), the Hutu government and extremist militias (Interahamwe) launched a coordinated campaign. Over the course of just 100 days, neighbors turned on neighbors, killing an estimated 800,000 Tutsis and moderate Hutus, primarily using machetes and small arms.

  • The International Failure: The United Nations and Western nations largely turned a blind eye, failing to intervene until the slaughter was already over.

docs.title: Unit 8.1   Setting the Stage for the Cold War (Ignoring Decolonization)
Meetings Between the Big Three
docs.text: The Big Three were the leaders of Great Britain, the Soviet Union, and the United States.

docs.title: November 1943: Tehran Conference
docs.text: Plans for freeing Europe from Nazi control - Soviet Union would free Eastern Europe and gain some Polish territory - Britain and the United States would free Western Europe

docs.title: February 1945: Yalta Conference
docs.text:
Plans for reconstructing Europe and defeating Japan - FDR wanted democratic elections and Soviets to join the war against Japan - Stalin wanted an Eastern European ‘buffer zone’ to prevent another Russian invasion. He also wanted Japanese islands, Chinese ports, and Manchurian railroads. Key Detail: The Soviet Union did not fully agree to free elections in Eastern Europe, but FDR believed that a potential war with the Soviet Union was deemed unpopular.

FDR dies on April 12, 1945 -> Replaced by Harry Truman Clement Atlee replaced Winston Churchill on July 26, 1945

docs.title: July 1945: Potsdam Conference
docs.text: Plans for the administration of post-Nazi Germany, as well as a strategy for ending the war against Japan - Truman also wanted democratic elections, but Stalin had already occupied Eastern Europe by then. Effect: Communists gained control of East Germany, Poland, Czechoslovakia, Hungary, Bulgaria, and Romania - Failed to settle major differences between the global superpowers, and so a Cold War Begins.

docs.title: Utter Destruction of Europe:
docs.text:
• Poland, Germany, and the Soviet Union lost between 10-20% of its population.
  • Immense physical destruction and population loss reduced global power from Europe, and the United States and the Soviet Union emerged as superpowers.
  • Eastern Europe suffered harder than Western Europe. Many educational institutions and innovative corporations remained intact in France and Britain.

docs.title: Growing Rivalry Between US and USSR:
docs.text:
• The United States suffered the least damage from WW2, and its industrial base grew stronger from military contracts.
  • The success from the United States allowed it to fund many aid programs to European nations.
  • The US were the frontrunners of the development of the atomic bomb, and the USSR only developed theirs in 1949.
  • Neither nation wanted a full-scale war with each other due to the heavy costs of WW2. They ‘fought’ via propaganda campaigns, arms races, and covert operations.
  • The US and USSR developed hydrogen bombs in the 1950s, more powerful than the atomic bomb.
  • Dwight D. Eisenhower warned against the military-industrial complex, for its unchecked power could drive financial incentives toward unnecessary conflict. Result: Citizens in many countries protested the stockpiling of nuclear weapons.

docs.title: Definition: Military-Industrial Complex
docs.text: The informal alliance between the government and the large defense contractors.

docs.title: Establishment of the United Nations
docs.text:
The League of Nations failed because of: - A lack of support from powerful nations, most notably the US. - There was no safeguard to quickly stop small conflicts to escalate into greater ones.

US, GB, USSR, and China discussed the establishment of the UN, and it was formally established in 1945.

Winston Churchill’s March 1946 speech:

docs.title: “An iron curtain has descended across the continent.”
docs.text: (This is where the establishment of the Iron Curtain metaphor begins.)

docs.title: Capitalism:
docs.text:
• Individual economic freedoms for people
  • Means of production like factories and businesses are owned by private individuals
  • Private owners make decisions about what to produce, when to product, and how much should it cost
  • People can compete in the market freely without any government assistance
  • Theoretically, prices will be minimized because producers choose the cheapest product that’s the best
  • Goal: “The most deserving person will usually win.”
  • Supply and Demand: price drop -> demand goes up and supply decreases; prices only rise as long as demand is high.

docs.title: Communism:
docs.text:
• No private ownership of property is allowed, Marx believed it encouraged greed.
  • Property should be shared.
  • “People” control the economy.
  • In the transition between capitalism and communism, the government should exercise control of the markets.
  • Goal: Eliminate wealth disparity between poor and rich.

docs.title: United States:
docs.text:
• elected leaders via free elections
  • independent press for accurate government info

docs.title: Soviet Union:
docs.text:
• insignificant elections
  • press operated by the government
  • dominated by a single party

docs.title: Similarities:
docs.text:
• Big economic decisions was in the hands of groups (either the government or millions of corporate shareholders).
  • Both countries acted out of fear of each other.
  • Both absolutely hated Nazis.

docs.title: Differences:
docs.text:
• USSR restricted freedom of speech and worship, free elections, and independent businesses.
  • USSR claimed the US gave people the “freedom to starve” and discriminating against minorities like African Americans.
  • USSR implemented equal rights for women.

docs.title: Establishment of Satellite States:
docs.text:
• The USSR enforced five-year economic plans in Eastern Europe to encourage the growth of industry and agriculture. Cost: Consumer products and all political parties besides Communism.
  • The satellite states were forced to import Soviet goods and export goods to the Soviet Union. Employed similar dictatorial governments.

docs.title: Definition - Satellite State:
docs.text:
small states that are economically or politically dependent on a larger, more powerful state.

After the October Revolution of 1917, the USSR saw capitalism as a global threat and developed the concept of a world revolution. Between 1917 and 1923, the USSR would support uprisings in Germany, Bavaria, Hungary, northern Italy, and Bulgaria.

docs.title: Containment:
docs.text: Some people supported the idea of violent overthrowing of regimes to destroy the spread of communism, while diplomat George Kennan (who worked in the US Embassy in Moscow between the 1930s and 1946) advocated for a policy of containment.

docs.title: March 1947 - Truman Doctrine:
docs.text:
• Harry Truman declared that the spread of communism must be stopped in Greece and Turkey.
  • The USSR wanted to put military bases in Turkey to control the Dardanelles Strait between the Black Sea and the Mediterranean.
  • Left-wing groups in Greece were controlled by communists.
  • Truman urged for economic and military support for these two nations (about $400 Million).

docs.title: June 1947 - Marshall Plan:
docs.text:
• For fear of the rise of Communism due to economic instability in European nations, America wanted to turn Europe into a stable and prosperous region.
  • America agreed to give $12 billion in aid to European nations, including Germany for modernization and repairment of damaged infrastructure.
  • Success: Economic output in aided countries was 35% higher in 1951 than in 1938.

docs.title: Jan 1949 - Council for Mutual Economic Assistance (COMECON):
docs.text:
• A plan initiated by the Soviet Union to economically aid Eastern Bloc nations and facilitate trade among socialist states.
  • The idea was to prevent Eastern European nations from accepting Western aid.
  • The members were originally Soviet Union, Bulgaria, Czechoslovakia, Hungary, Poland, and Romania, but Albania joined in 1949 and East Germany joined in 1950.

docs.title: Space Race:
docs.text:
• 1957: The Soviet Union launches the first Earth-orbiting satellite (Sputnik 1) and later the first organism in space (Sputnik 2, Laika the dog).
  • 1958: The US launches their first satellite (Explorer 1). The National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) is also created in this year.
  • 1961: Yuri Gagarin becomes the first man in space. Alan Shepard becomes the first American in space shortly after.
  • 1963: The USSR sends the first woman into space (Valentina Tereshkova).
  • 1969: Neil Armstrong and Edwin “Buzz” Aldrin become the first men to walk on the moon.

In 1975, tensions soften between the US and USSR as they have a joint mission, in which commanders Tom Stafford and Alexei Leonov have an ‘international handshake,’ ending the Space Race.

docs.title: Mutually Assured Destruction:
docs.text: • In 1959, the US and USSR had developed intercontinental ballistic missiles capable of delivering warheads into each other’s territory. These nations realized that nuclear war would lead to total destruction, leading to a mutually assured destruction. Result: No incentive to start war.

docs.title: Non-Aligned Movement:
docs.text:
• Many African and Asian countries wanted to not be involved with the Cold War, and wanted an alternative framework for political and economic order.
  • 1955 - Bandung Conference:
    • delegates from more than 27 countries came (more than 1/2 of world pop.)
    • Catalyst for the formal creation of the Non-Aligned Movement in 1961.

docs.title: Problems with the Non-Aligned Movement:
docs.text:
• In times of crises, many nations would align with either the US or USSR.
    • Example: 1977 war between Somalia and Ethiopia: USSR backed Ethiopia, prompting US to back Somalia.
  • Member states tried to use international institutions to project their own interests.
    • Example: Jawaharlal Nehru supported a stronger UN, but opposed efforts to intervene in Kashmir.

docs.title: The Division of Germany
docs.text:
1945 - Germany and Berlin were divided into occupational zones between the Allies and the USSR.

1948 - After the Marshall Plan, the Allies (France, US, and Great Britain) introduced the Deutschmark currency to counter the worthless Reichsmark, which the Soviets refused to accept. The Soviets then withdrew from the Allied Control Council.

docs.title: June 1948 - May 1949: Berlin Blockade
docs.text:
• USSR severed water, railroad, and road access to Western sectors of Berlin.
  • To avoid military confrontation, the Allies established the Berlin Airlift, and between February 1948 and May 1949, the Allies flew supplies into Western Zones.

docs.title: Formation of NATO:
docs.text:
The blockade also solidified Western unity, and alongside a growing idea to form a coordinated plan to stop Soviet expansion, Western Allies formed the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) in April 1949. Original Members: Belgium, Canada, Denmark, France, Great Britain, Iceland, Italy, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Norway, Portugal, US.

1949 - The Allies (France, US, and Great Britain) agreed to formally establish the nations of West Germany and East Germany.

Between 1949 and 1961, around 2.5 million East Germans fled to the West in search for democracy. Since this mass fleeing demonstrated weakness in communist systems, the borders were heavily patrolled and around 150 people were killed. Barbed wires became an actual wall, known as the Berlin Wall, formed in August 1961.

The fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 demonstrated a new birth in European democracy as well as the end of Soviet influence.

docs.title: Warsaw Pact:
docs.text:
• The Warsaw Pact was the USSR’s response to NATO, established in 1955.
  • Members: Albania, Bulgaria, Czechoslovakia, East Germany, Hungary, Poland, Romania, USSR

**1954 - Formation of the Southeast Asia Treaty Organization (SEATO) - Members: Australia, France, Great Britain, New Zealand, Pakistan, Philippines, Thailand, US - Aimed to prevent the spread of Communism in Southeast Asia.

**1959 - Formation of the Central Treaty Organization (CENTO) - Members: Iran, Iraq, Pakistan, Turkey -> Iraq eventually withdrew - Aimed to prevent the spread of Communism in the Middle East.

docs.title: Resistance:
docs.text:
• Albania withdrew in 1968 as it became more closely tied with China.
  • Yugoslavia, under the leadership of Marshall Josip Broz Tito never joined the Warsaw Pact. However, Yugoslavia split in the 1990s.

docs.title: Definition - Proxy War
docs.text:
An indirect conflict where major powers fuel battles by assisting smaller nations to fight on their behalf.

The threat of nuclear war kept the US and USSR from direct, global destruction, but nonetheless, proxy wars between the US and USSR still resulted in the deaths of millions.

docs.title: Korean War (1950 - 1953):
docs.text:
• 1950 - North Korea attempts to invade South Korea to reunite under its leadership. UN responds by voting to defend South Korea militarily. Soviets did not choose to veto due to a protest in Security Council meetings over China’s seat.
  • The North was lead by Kim Il-Sung (communist government) and the South was lead by Syngman Rhee, an authoritarian leader backed by the US.
    • 16 member countries militarily aided South Korea.
  • General Douglas MacArthur was the lead commander for the US, directing the Inchon landing, the defense of the Busan perimeter, and the invasion of North Korea.
    • MacArthur hinted at the use of nuclear weapons or attacking China, and so Truman dismissed him for insubordination on April 1951.

docs.title: Definition - Limited War
docs.text:
A war in which the weapons used, the nations or territory involved, or the objectives pursued are restricted in some way, in particular one in which the use of nuclear weapons is avoided.

  • Soviets aided NK economically rather than militarily, but China joined in out of concern that the UN peacekeeping forces would try to invade it.
  • STALEMATE - establishment of the demilitarized zone.

Backlink to a page about Vietnam, demonstrating both decolonization and proxy war between US and USSR via the Vietnam War.

Backlink to a page about Cuba, demonstrating the Bay of Pigs and the Cuban Missile Crisis.

Backlink to a page about Examples of Deescalating Tensions Between Capitalist and Communist Powers, in which nuclear powers sought to ban testing of nuclear weapons in non-underground areas Nuclear Test-Ban Treaty (1963) and prevent the spread of nuclear materials to non-nuclear countries Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (1968).

docs.title: Angola:
docs.text:
• After around 400 years of Portuguese rule, resistance against colonialism started around 1961, starting the Angolan Independence War (1961-1974), lead by three main groups.
    • MPLA (Popular Movement for the Liberation of Angola)
      • Marxist-aligned, supported by USSR and Cuba
    • FNLA (National Front for the Liberation of Angola)
      • Supported by US and Zaire
    • UNITA (National Union for the Total Independence of Angola)
      • Aided by South Africa and the US
  • Angola formally received independence on November 11, 1975. However, the MPLA took control of the capital, Luanda, on the same month.
  • The Angolan Civil War lasted from 1975 to 2002, with conflict between the MPLA and UNITA.
    • large destruction to infrastructure
    • large social inequalities between urban and rural areas
    • land ownership disputes (still ongoing)
    • ceasefire after death of UNITA leader

docs.title: Contra War, Nicaragua:
docs.text:
• The 43 year dictatorship by the Somoza family was ended by the rebel Sandinistas, self-proclaimed socialists (1979).
  • 1981 - The Contras, conservative opponents of the Sandinistas, tried to overthrow them, leading to the Contra War (1981-1988).
    • The US heavily supported the Contras since the Sandinistas were socialist. The USSR provided nearly $3 billion in military equipment to aid the Sandinista. Cuba also helped them.

Backlink to the antinuclear weapons movement.

docs.title: Unit 8.4   Spread of Communism After 1900
docs.text:
Backlink to the entire history of China, which describes: Mao Zedong communist victory five year plans Great Leap Forward Cultural Revolution and Red Guards Punishment of the Red Four ‘Destalinization’ after 1953, China starts establishing relations with US

We will now discuss land reform movements as a tool for economic change and even political change.

docs.title: Iran:
docs.text:
• Britain viewed Persia as a crucial buffer state to protect India during the 19th century, and the weak Qatar Dynasty gave economic abilities to foreign powers (such as Britain gaining control of their oil).
  • Iran’s leader considered support Hitler, which prompted a Russian and British invasion. This led to power being transferred to the leader’s son, Shah Muhammad Reza Pahlavi.
  • 1951 - Nationalists forced Shah Pahlavi to flee the country, and selected Mohammad Mosaddegh as PM in 1953.
    • He wanted to nationalize oil companies, which prompted the US and GB to overthrow Mosaddegh and return the shah to power.
  • The shah ruled harshly under an authoritarian regime, but instituted progressive reforms known as the White Revolution (due to lack of bloodshed).
    • Women suffrage
    • Social welfare
    • Literacy programs
    • Land ownership - The government bought land from landlords and resold to peasants for a cheaper price.
      • Not that effective as not many peasants had this offer available to them.
      • Landowners and frustrated peasants both opposed these land reforms, and religious conservatives opposed modernization, leading to opposition against the Shah.
  • Iranian Revolution (1979):
    • Shah ousted from power
    • Islamic theocracy put into power
    • Government lead by a Guardian Council that interpreted constitution based on shariah law.
    • Also led by a clergy, that could approve/disapprove anyone running for office.
    • Anti-western, anti-Israel

docs.title: Venezuela Land-Reforms
docs.text:
• Venezuela sough to end large estate ownership and redistribute lands to peasants.
  • Redistribution of 5 million acres of land: some from state government, some taken from landowners. Problems:
  • lack of support for farmers to actually make the land productive -> abandoned projects
  • some landowners saw this as a violation of human rights
  • illegal squatters sometimes moved in to settle on these lands
  • Political divide: For many Venezuelans, the person they would vote for is based on if they support their point of view on land reforms.

docs.title: Slight Historical Context:
docs.text:
• 1930s - The United Fruit Company (UFCO) controlled almost half of Guatemala’s land, and many of it was unused to prevent other competitors from buying it.
    • They owned rail lines and telegraph systems.
  • The UFCO received lots of land concessions from previous Guatemalan dictators in exchange for low wages.

Unit 8 Context: - 1954 - Democratically elected President Jacobo Arbenz sought to redistribute this unused land to peasants. - The UFCO then lobbed the US government to remove Arbenz, and the CIA eventually overthrew him. - Results: The land reform was reversed, and revolutionary ideas started spreading, eventually leading to the Guatemalan Civil War (1960-1966).

docs.title: Vietnam Land Reform
docs.text: Backlink to the Vietnam page and how North Vietnam tried to redistribute land from large landowners to peasants.

docs.title: Ethiopia Land Reform
docs.text:
• After a struggle against the Italian fascist invasion (1935-1941), Haile Selassie returns to power from exile during WW2.
    • Politically Western alignment
    • Economic success from coffee trade
    • Centralization of power
    • Stronger international affairs
      • Western political and economic reforms
    • Problem: Selassie’s power depended on the support of the land-owning aristocracy and the Ethiopian Orthodox Church. Therefore, he could not implement much land reforms.
    • Military leaders eventually deposed Selassie in 1974, and Mengistu Haile Mariam soon rose to power.
      • Assassinated members of the old regime
      • Established a socialist government, receiving aid from the Soviet Union and other communist countries.
      • Problems: Famine and bad economic policies forced Mengstu to flee to Zimbabwe in 1971.

docs.title: India Land Reform
docs.text: After India’s independence in 1947 and its subsequent partition, India undertook some economic reforms. - Abolished intermediary rent collectors (Zamindari System) - Protected tenants from random evictions - Limits on land a family could own resulted in surplus land distributed across landless farmers, demonstrating cooperative farming India saw mixed results in land reform.

docs.title: Kerala
docs.text: In 1957, Kerala democratically elected a communist government. - 1963: Tenants won the right to purchase land form landowners - 1969: Tenants won the right to become full owners of land - 1974: Laws allowed for minimum wages. The central government was not politically aligned with Kerala’s communist government (as the former sided with landowners), and so they eventually took direct rule of Kerala to reverse land reforms.

docs.title: Negotiated Independence - India
docs.text:
As India was “Britain’s most valuable colony,” Britain invested a lot of money towards its infrastructure, leading towards modernization. Although this modernization assisted the British, it helped establish a growing Indian middle class that was influenced by nationalism and self-determination.

1885 - India forms the Indian National Congress to petition the British government for a greater voice in politics. Result: Many petitions were largely ignored.

Even after millions of Indian troops served in WW1, Britain did not entertain hopes of self-determination. Crowds of nonviolent protesters, like ones in the Amritsar Massacre (1919) were often met with harsh suppression. After Mohandas gandhi became leader in 1920, the resistance movement was then seen as nonviolent.

India demands independence after WW2, and since there were many pro-independence politicians in parliament, Britain recognized an independent India in 1947. However, Britain also recognized an independent Muslim Pakistan in 1947. Note: The establishment of India as a new state was met with immense violence. 1906 - Muslims form the Muslim League and called for their own state separate from India. Muslim and Hindu extremists committed harsh crimes against each other during the Partition of India, and around a million people were killed during this transition.

docs.title: Slight Context:
docs.text:
• WW2 Gold Coast Veterans were not happy with colonial conditions
  • 1948 Accra Riots - many political leaders, like Kwame Nkrumah, were killed, and protesters were shot
  • 1949 - Nkrumah forms the Convention People’s Party (CPP) and organized protests and strikes via a nonviolent manner.

The Gold Coast Colony and British Togoland granted independence from Britain, and these territories were later renamed ‘Ghana.’

docs.title: Nkrumah’s Leadership
docs.text:
• Nkrumah became the first president of Ghana in 1960.
  • Nkrumah took inspiration from many nationalist states like the US and created a nation that reflected on its rich past (hence the name change to Ghana), establishing currency, the flag, and museums.
  • Nkrumah also initiated many public projects like hydroelectric plants.
  • Later became corrupt and accused of running the country into debt, establishing a one party state in 1964.

Nkrumah was a proud supporter of Pan-Africanism, which was the celebration of African ideas across the continent. Eventually, he founded the Organization of African Unity in 1963. In 1966, Nkrumah’s government was overtaken by a military coup.

  • Ghana eventually created a new constitution in 1992 to have more democratically fair elections.

docs.title: Violent Independence - Algeria
docs.text:
• Algerians started to campaign for independence after WW2, which lead to the beginning of the Algerian War for Independence in 1954.
  • Many French people settled in Algeria, so France decided that Algeria could not break off as a separate colony.
  • The National Liberation Front, or FLN, utilized guerrilla techniques against 500 thousand French troops. Result: hundreds of thousands of Algerias were tortured and/or killed.

  • French President Charles de Gaulle eventually planned a referendum for Algeria in which the people of France and Algeria voted, and independence was granted on 1962.

  • Anti-immigration sentiment grew as pro-French Algerians and settlers fled back to France.

  • Algerian violence saw between 50K to 150K killed by the FLN and lynch mobs.
  • A military coup replaced the first president of the Algerian Republic in 1965, and the FLN maintained a socialist authoritarian order.
    • On the flip side, agriculture was collectivized and industry was modernized.
  • After the Islamic Salvation Front won an election, violence broke out between the FLN and the latter due to a violation of the one-party rule, beginning the Algerian Civil War (1991-2002). The military state of emergency was finally lifted in 2011.
  • Islamist movements challenge Algeria’s growth, as they were keen on using violence to gain popularity. Algeria banned religion-based parties in 1997.

docs.title: Negotiated Independence in French West Africa
docs.text: France gave a relatively swift independence to many West African colonies during the mid-1950s, this was mostly due to the fact that there were not much French settlers in these regions. - France then invested in these regions by inducing agricultural growth and better trade.

docs.title: Violent Independence - Vietnam
docs.text: Backlink to Post WW2 Vietnam#Unit 8.5 - Decolonization After 1900.

docs.title: Egypt and the Suez Crisis
docs.text:
• General Gamal Abdel Nasser and Muhammad Naguib overthrows the king and replaces it with an Egyptian Republic.
  • Nasser strongly supported Pan-Arabism, the movement that promoted political and cultural unity between Arab nations.
  • Nasser employed socialism by turning private farms into systems that implemented irrigation and drainage with the sharing of profit from crops (land reforms).
    • Nasser also nationalized some industries and foreign owned banks, eventually nationalizing the Suez Canal.
  • 1956 - Nasser seized the Suez Canal, which was administered by both the French and the British, which led to a convert plan between France and Israel to invade Egypt, which eventually involved the UN to resolve the Suez Crisis.
  • For peacekeeping measures, the US later became involved, and UN peacekeepers were stationed at the sinai peninsula until an agreement was made to open the Suez Canal to the world. We also see that Egypt maintains a “non-aligned stance” between US and USSR here.

docs.title: Independence and Civil War in Nigeria
Quebec’s Quiet Revolution
docs.text:
• Quebec was formerly a territory of France, called New France, and it was mostly Catholic compared to British Canada’s Protestantism.
  • Quebec became more aligned with France, and in the 1960s, a Quiet Revolution ensued, in which the Liberal Party started making economic reforms that supported separatist movements.
  • Some nationalist groups used terrorism as a means of awareness.
  • PM Pierre Trudeau was a native of Quebec, easing tensions.
  • 1995 - A referendum to make Quebec independent failed.

docs.title: Israel
docs.text:
1890s - Zionism movement born as a response to the Dreyfus Affair (French Jewish officer Alfred Dreyfus was falsely accused of giving information to Germany). Theodor Herzl formed the Zionist movement.

Zionist demand a state in Palestine as Jews historically lived in that region in the past, but Palestine was already inhabited and was mostly Arab Muslims.

1917 - Balfour Declaration: Arthur Balfour writes a letter to Lord Rothschild, where Britain promises a national home for the Jewish people while promising Arab leaders an independent state in the region. 1918 - The collapse of the Ottoman Empire allowed European Jews to be brought to Palestine, and Britain subsequently gave the Israel-Palestine administration to the hands of the UN.

1948 - The UN divides Palestine into Jewish and Arab sections, thus forming Israel.

docs.title: 1956: Response to the Suez Crisis
