docs.text: Israel, backed by France and Great Britain, invades the Sinai Peninsula in part to liberate the Suez Canal.

docs.title: 1967: Six Day War:
docs.text: Israel gains Egypt’s Gaza Strip, the West Bank, Jordan’s East Jerusalem, and Syria’s Golan Heights.

docs.title: 1973: Yom-Kippur War
docs.text:
Israel successfully defeats Egypt and Syria against their surprise invasion.

The Palestinian Liberation Organization (PLO) rejects the Camp David Accords (which offered peace between Israel and Egypt) due to the desire to the return of occupied lands. The PLO was lead by Yasser Arafat.

docs.title: Context:
docs.text:
• After Japan's surrender in WW2, France took back the colony of French Indochina (which included Cambodia).
  • To gain international support for Cambodian independence, King Sihanouk goes into "self-imposed exile" in 1953 and tours Western nations like the US.
  • Cambodia formally became independent on November 1953. It tried to maintain a non-aligned status, but was pressured by the North Vietnamese army to join the Vietnam War to use its land as supply routes.
  • In 1970, Sihanouk was overthrown by pro-American General Lon Nol.

Eventually, a communist guerrilla organization called the Khmer Rouge took power (leader: Pol Pot). It's form of communism was ruthless and implemented a system similar to China's cultural revolution, targeting intellectuals.

docs.title: Definition:
docs.text:
Brain drain - the emigration of highly trained or intelligence people from a particular country

Cambodia had one of the severest instances of brain drain in world history. It was left with almost no doctors, teachers, or experts. As a result, Cambodia became one of the poorest nations in the world.

2 Million Deaths - 1/4th of Cambodia's pop.

1977 - Vietnam invades Cambodia to stop Pol Pot's Regime and the Khmer Rouge. Result: Vietnamese Victory. Vietnam helped Cambodia reach some internal stability and completely withdrew all forces in 1989.

Prince Sihanouk returned to Cambodia as a constitutional monarch in 1993 and Cambodia started to implement democratic policies.

(Include Backlink to South Asia)

docs.title: Tanzania
docs.text:
• Tanzania gained independence from Britain in 1961.
  • Julius Nyerere became the first president of the United Republic of Tanzania and emphasized self-reliance and traditional African values.

1967 Arusha Declaration - a document of Tanzania's political and economic identity. Key Points: - self-reliance -> the country would mostly rely on land and people and not foreign aid to avoid neo-colonialism. - The elimination of class divisions (part of Ujamaa, Swahili for "familyhood"). - Development should be measured by the well-being of people (ex. education and health), rather than by GDP. - Forbade officials from owning shares in companies -> eliminating the potential of a ruling elite.

Successes: - Implemented Swahili as a national language, unifying over 120 ethnic groups. - Tanzania achieved one of the highest literacy rates in Africa. - Access to clean water and rural clinics increased significantly, so life expectancy also increased.

Failures: - The forced collectivization of farms was often resisted and was highly inefficient. - By the late 1970's, self-reliance dropped and Tanzania became heavily dependent on foreign aid. - State corporations were often mismanaged.

Nyerere resigned the presidency in 1985.

docs.title: Definition:
docs.text:
Metropole - a large city of a former colonial ruler

Examples: London - people from India, Pakistan, East Africa, and the Caribbean moved to London for better opportunities and work. Paris - After the Algerian War and the decolonization of West Africa (Senegal, Mali, Ivory Coast), large numbers of migrants moved to Paris. Amsterdam - Significant migration occurred from former Dutch East Indies territories and Suriname. - Impact -> Dutch cuisine has Indonesian and Surinamese influences.

docs.title: Mohandas Gandhi:
docs.text:
• developed a philosophy of nonviolent resistance called Satyagraha (meaning devotion to truth/civil disobedience).
  • Salt March (1930) - Led a 240-mile march to the sea to protest the British monopoly on salt.
  • Homespun Movement - Encouraged Indians to spin their own cloth to boycott British textiles. India eventually gained independence in 1947.

docs.title: Martin Luther King Jr.
docs.text:
• A prominent leader of the American Civil Rights Movement.
  • Montgomery Bus Boycott - Led a 381-day boycott of the city's bus system after Rosa Parks was arrested.
  • March on Washington (1963) - Delivered his "I Have a Dream" speech to advocate for an end to racism and for better civil rights. America eventually passed the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965.

docs.title: Definition:
docs.text:
Apartheid - a policy or system of segregation or discrimination on grounds of race.

  • Originally a non-violent protestor with the African National Congress, but later sabotaged government property after the state starting enacting violence on peaceful protestors.
  • He eventually spent 27 years in prison.
  • After his release in 1990, he negotiated the end of Apartheid and became South Africa's first black president in 1994.

docs.title: Challenges to Soviet Power in Eastern Europe
docs.text:
• (1956) Poland - workers demonstrated against Soviets and demanded better living conditions. Result: The collectivization of farms ended and a new secretary of the Polish Communist Party, Wladyslaw Gomulka, pursued independent domestic policy in Poland.
  • (1956) Hungary - Leader Imre Nagy declared freedom from Soviet control and demanded Soviets troops to evacuate. He was a supporter for free elections and advocated for neutrality. Result: Soviet invasion and execution.

docs.title: Czechoslovakia (Prague Spring of 1968):
docs.text:
• Leader Alexander Dubcek introduced "Socialism with a human face" that included freedoms of speech and press.
  • Fearing the spread of democracy, the Soviet Union and Warsaw Pact allies invaded Czechoslovakia in August 1968.
  • The Brezhnev Doctrine: Following this event, the USSR established a policy stating that the Soviet Union had the right to intervene in any Eastern Bloc country where communism was under threat.

docs.title: France:
docs.text:
Hundreds of thousands of students marched in the streets, and around 10 million workers went on strike.

Other:

docs.title: Terrorism as an Act of Resistance:
docs.text:
• Northern Ireland: All of Ireland except Northern Ireland gained independence from the UK in 1922. There was tension between Catholics (that supported independence) and Protestants that refused. Around 3,500 died in their conflict, and some members of the Irish Republican Army (Catholic supported) used terrorism to achieve political ends (like setting off bombs in London).
  • Spain: The Basque region in Spain wanted independence and formed the Basque Homeland and Freedom organization. The ETA killed more than 800 people, and killed dictator Francisco Franco in 1973. It declared an end to violent actions in 2011.
  • Peru: Former philosophy professor Abimael Guzman built a Mao-inspired organization called Shining Path that performed many assassinations to overthrow the existing government. Around 37K people were killed and continued attacks into the late 90s.
  • (Extremist) Islamic Terrorism:
    • They had a fundamentalist interpretation of Islam and most Muslims condemned them.
    • Most victims were Muslims, and many cities like Madrid, London, and Paris were attacked.
    • Examples: The Taliban in Afghanistan, Al-Qaeda's attack during 9/11

docs.title: Response of Militarized States to Internal Conflicts:
Francisco Franco's Spain:
docs.text:
• The Conflict: Ethnic minorities (like Basques and Catalans) and political dissidents (socialists, communists, and liberals) challenged his centralized, traditionalist rule.
  • The Response: Franco used his military and secret police to execute or imprison hundreds of thousands of "enemies of the state." He banned regional languages and used the military to enforce strict cultural and political conformity.

docs.title: Uganda's Idi Amin:
docs.text:
• The Conflict: Internal opposition from different ethnic groups and those loyal to the previous president (Milton Obote).
  • The Response: Known as the "Butcher of Uganda," Amin used "State Research Bureau" (military death squads) to purge his own army and civilian population. He also expelled the entire Asian (Indian and Pakistani) minority population in 1972, seizing their businesses to give to his military supporters.

docs.title: Brazil (Military Dictatorship 1964-1985):
docs.text: In 1964, a US-backed military coup overthrew the Brazilian government to prevent what they feared was a communist lean. - The Conflict: Student activists, labor unions, and urban guerrillas protested the military's suspension of constitutional rights. - The Response: The military government passed Institutional Act No. 5, which suspended habeas corpus, legalized torture of political prisoners, and censored all media. The military controlled the judiciary and used "disappearances" to silence critics.

docs.title: Unit 8.8   End of the Cold War
Definition:
docs.text:
Detente - the easing of hostility or strained relations, especially between countries.

The US and USSR entered a period of detente after the Bay of Pigs and the Cuban Missile Crisis.

1972, Strategic Arms Limitation Treaty (SALT): Richard Nixon went to the Soviet Union and visited Soviet leader Leonid Brezhnev to sign SALT. This aimed to freeze the number of ICBM's that each power could keep.

1972, Nixon's Visit to China: As Nixon visited China, the USSR became fearful that the U.S. and China would partner up, resulting in more cooperation between the Soviets and Americans to prevent isolation (triangular diplomacy). Nixon also did this because the American economy suffered from the Vietnam War.

1975 Helsinki Accords: A massive diplomatic agreement signed by 35 nations (including US and USSR) for the USSR to respect human rights and basic freedoms and recognize post-WW2 borders of Eastern Europe.

Trade of Grain: - The US gave excess grain to the Soviet Union, benefitting American farmers (access to a large, new market) and the people of the USSR. - After the Soviets invaded Afghanistan in 1979, Jimmy Carter stopped grain shipments, ending detente.

docs.title: Soviet-Afghan War (1979-1989):
docs.text:
• The USSR wanted to raise communist influence against Muslim fighters in the region.
  • They could not defeat the guerrilla groups in the rough terrain, thus undermining Soviet legitimacy.
  • Around 562K to 2 million Afghani civilians died, and many fled to Pakistan and Iran.

docs.title: The Rise of US and USSR Tensions, Again
docs.text:
• Reagan sent military aid to support the Afghans against the USSR.
  • Both the US and USSR had more than 12K missiles.

Strategic Defense Initiative: - Reagan created a system that would destroy any Soviet nukes that attacked the United States or its allies. - The Soviets lacked such a system and moderate Soviets started demanding reforms, having economic concerns and saw Reagan's initiative as the start to an arms race in space. - Nations believed that they now had to choose sides between the US and USSR.

docs.title: End of the Soviet Union
docs.text:
• Mikhail Gorbachev rose to power in 1985, favoring perestroika (attempts to restructure economy) and glasnost (policy of opening of and increasing freedoms). Reagan particularly liked Gorbachev and met three times in two years.
  • The Warsaw Pact started to fall as Gorbachev ended economic support to Soviet satellites in Eastern Europe. These countries started getting more exposed to Western democracy, leading to the Fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989.
  • Lithuania, Georgia, and other Soviet republics started to overthrow their rulers, and the USSR dissolved in December 1991, forming many countries (the strongest being Russia).

docs.title: Unit 9.2   Technological Advances and Limitations After 1900   Disease
Overview:
docs.text:
• New scientific and medicinal developments
  • Pandemics: epidemic diseases that spread internationally
  • Diseases related to poverty (malaria, cholera, tuberculosis), epidemics (HIV/AIDS, Ebola), and diseases related to increased longevity (heart disease, Alzheimers)

docs.title: 1918 Influenza Pandemic:
docs.text:
• 50M+ people killed, spread across the US (from Kansas)
  • Spread globally as American troops arrived in France (cuz WW1 obv)
  • Baseline Symptoms: fever, chills, sore throat, then newer symptoms like black feet, and "red lungs drowning in liquid"
  • Called "Spanish Flu" because Spanish media was uncensored during WW1, so it reported accurate numbers and was thought of as the country of origin. Impact: World economy collapsed because people aged 18-40 died of disease. Change: Women joined the workforce, then gained the right to vote in 1920.

docs.title: HIV/Aids:
docs.text:
• Human Immunodeficiency Virus
  • attacks cells that help fend off pathogens
  • Causes: Unprotected sex, blood transfer, needles
  • Not curable but treatable
  • AIDS is a later stage of HIV that's more severe
  • Antiretroviral drugs developed around 1990 to stop HIV but was expensive.
  • Ethnic minorities, homosexual communities, and Southern African countries are most affected.

docs.title: Cholera:
docs.text:
• Bacterial disease spread via contaminated water.
  • Effects: Forced countries to innovate better standard for cleaning water, and set up modern sanitation methods of water today.
  • Prevalent in countries lacking WASH - water, sanitation, and hygiene
  • Countries with environmental changes like floods are at greater risk.

docs.title: Alzheimers:
docs.text:
• Nature: A neurodegenerative disorder characterized by progressive memory loss and cognitive decline.
  • Biology: Marked by extracellular Amyloid Beta plaques and intracellular Tau tangles.
  • History: Originally thought to be a rare form of dementia until Robert Katzman’s 1976 paper identified it as a major killer and the 4th leading cause of death.

docs.title: Heart Disease:
docs.text:
• Context: Became the leading cause of death globally as life expectancy increased.
  • Causes: Linked to genetics, lifestyle choices, smoking, and dietary changes.
  • Medical Advances:
    • First heart transplant (1967) by Christiaan Barnard.
    • First artificial heart (1982) by Robert Jarvik’s team.

docs.title: Ebola:
docs.text:
• Source: A virus originating in African fruit bats, transmitted to humans through fluids.
  • Outbreaks: Significant spike in cases and deaths during the 2014 outbreak.

docs.title: Tuberculosis:
docs.text:
• Nature: A highly contagious bacterial infection typically affecting the lungs.
  • Symptoms: Persistent cough (sometimes with blood), fever, night sweats, and weight loss.
  • Evolution of Treatment:
    • Early: Quarantine and exposure to sunlight.
    • Modern: Use of specific antibiotics like Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, and Ethambutol.

docs.title: Malaria:
docs.text:
• Cause: A parasite spread by mosquitoes in tropical areas such as Africa and the Amazon.
  • Impact: Kills over 600,000 people annually; historically, it accounted for 2–5% of all 20th-century deaths.
  • Demographics: Predominantly affects young children under age 5 (accounting for ~75% of deaths).
  • Persistence: Remains a threat due to poor housing, contaminated water, and limited healthcare in impoverished regions.
  • Prevention: Includes mosquito nets treated with insecticide and vaccines.

Poverty-Related Diseases: Malaria, Cholera, Tuberculosis Emergent Epidemics and Pandemics: Influenza, HIV/AIDS, Ebola Diseases Associated with Longevity: Heart Disease, Alzheimer's

docs.title: Unit 9.3   Environmental Impacts in the Global Age
1. Causes of the Global Environmental Crisis
docs.text:
The 20th and 21st centuries saw unprecedented environmental change driven by globalization and industrialization.

  • Industrial Activity: Large-scale manufacturing has led to widespread environmental contamination.

  • Resource Extraction: Illegal operations like logging, mining, and hunting have caused massive environmental destruction and species endangerment.

  • Agricultural Practices: Overfarming and deforestation have depleted natural resources.

  • Carbon Emissions: Increased use of fossil fuels has significantly raised carbon emissions.

    • Top global emitters include companies like Saudi Aramco, Chevron, Gazprom, and ExxonMobil.

  • Political Factors: Lax environmental legislation and intense corporate lobbying have hindered protective measures.

docs.title: 2. Deforestation and Biodiversity Loss
docs.text:
• Population Pressure: A rapidly increasing global population has created a need for more living space, leading to the clearing of forests.

  • Loss of Habitat: Since forests are critical habitats, their destruction has caused many species to become endangered or extinct, resulting in significant biodiversity loss.

  • Climate Link: Trees act as carbon sinks; their removal accelerates climate change by leaving more CO2 in the atmosphere.

docs.title: 3. Climate Change and Melting Ice Caps
docs.text:
• Greenhouse Gases: Human-induced climate change is driven by the rise of gases such as carbon dioxide (CO2), methane, and nitrous oxide.

  • Melting Ice Sheets: Global warming is causing ice caps to melt worldwide.

  • Consequences:

    • Habitat Loss: Species living on polar ice (e.g., polar bears) are losing their environments.

    • Sea Level Rise: Melting ice leads to rising seawater levels and increased global flooding.

  • Historical Context: Much of the current crisis is traced back to technological advances from the Industrial Revolution, including cars, factories, and the high demand for modern consumer goods like plastic and electronics.

docs.title: 4. The Ozone Hole Crisis
docs.text:
• The Ozone Layer: A layer of O3 (oxygen) that protects the Earth from harmful solar radiation.

  • Discovery: In 1985, scientists formally recognized a hole forming in the ozone layer over Antarctica.

  • Cause: 1970s technologies like refrigeration and aerosol deodorants used chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs).

    • When CFCs are hit by UV light, they break down ozone faster than it can naturally replenish.

  • Montreal Protocol (1987): An international treaty widely adopted to phase out CFCs; it is often cited as the most successful example of global environmental cooperation.

docs.title: 5. Responses and Movements
docs.text:
• Political Action: Green Parties have formed in nations like the US and UK to lobby for stronger environmental laws.

  • Green Belt Movement (1977): Founded by Wangari Maathai in Kenya, this movement focuses on teaching women to plant trees to combat deforestation and desertification.

  • International Agreements:

    • Kyoto Protocol (1997) and Paris Agreement (2015) aimed to address global climate change through international cooperation.

  • Greenpeace: An NGO founded in 1971 that uses localized activism to push for corporate accountability and better legislation.

  • Proposed Policies: The "Green New Deal" in the US is a proposed framework to mitigate environmental issues through policy change.

  • Public Awareness: Climate strikes, scientific outreach, and documentaries have reinforced the perceived threat of environmental collapse.

docs.title: 6. Sustainable Alternatives: Renewable Energy
docs.text:
Global efforts have shifted toward using energy sources that are replenished at a higher rate than they are consumed.

  • Benefits: Renewable sources create far lower emissions and are more environmentally friendly than fossil fuels.

  • Examples: Solar, wind, geothermal, hydropower, and bioenergy.

docs.title: Unit 9.4   Economics in the Global Age
1. Neoliberalism and Free-Market Policies
docs.text:
After the Cold War, many Western nations moved toward Neoliberalism, an economic approach inspired by laissez-faire capitalism.

  • Key Figures: Margaret Thatcher (UK) and Ronald Reagan (US).

  • Core Ideas:

    • Reduction of government regulation and influence on the economy.

    • Tax breaks and financial stimulation for large corporations.

    • "Trickle-Down Economics": The belief that financial benefits given to the wealthy and corporations will eventually "trickle down" to the rest of society through job creation and investment.

docs.title: 2. Agents of Liberalization
docs.text:
Specific leaders pushed for free-market reforms, often through drastic measures:

  • Augusto Pinochet (Chile): Led a US-backed coup; implemented "Chicago School" economic policies that privatized assets and opened Chile to global trade.

  • Deng Xiaoping (China): Though a communist leader, he opened China to the global market via "Socialism with Chinese Characteristics," allowing for Special Economic Zones and foreign investment.

docs.title: 3. Global Economic Institutions
docs.text:
To stabilize the world economy after WWII and the Cold War, several international organizations were created:

  • World Trade Organization (WTO):

    • Replaced the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT).

    • Aims to facilitate smooth, predictable, and free trade.

    • Criticism: Critics argue it favors developed nations over developing ones.

  • World Bank & International Monetary Fund (IMF):

    • Provide loans and financial assistance to struggling countries.

    • Often require "Structural Adjustment Programs" (austerity measures) in return for aid.

docs.title: 4. Regional Trade Agreements
docs.text:
Nations formed blocs to eliminate tariffs and trade barriers to boost regional economies:

  • NAFTA (North American Free Trade Agreement):

    • Created a free-trade zone between the US, Canada, and Mexico.

    • Later replaced by the USMCA.

    • Controversy: While it increased trade, some argue it led to the loss of manufacturing jobs in the US and exploited cheaper labor in Mexico.

  • Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN): Promotes economic growth and regional stability in Southeast Asia.

docs.title: 5. Multinational Corporations (MNCs)
docs.text:
MNCs are businesses that operate in multiple countries, wielding immense economic and political power.

  • Examples: Nestlé, Nissan, Mahindra & Mahindra.

  • Case Study: Nestlé:

    • Pros: Invests in sustainable agricultural methods and trains farmers.

    • Cons: Has faced heavy criticism for using child labor in cocoa production and for commodifying water (treating it as a product rather than a human right).

docs.title: 6. Knowledge Economies
docs.text:
In the late 20th century, the global economy shifted toward "Knowledge Economies," where growth is driven by technical and scientific innovation.

  • Key Sectors: IT, pharmaceuticals, and education.

  • Geographic Shift: Manufacturing moved to developing nations (like Vietnam or Bangladesh), while developed nations focused on design, software development, and services.

docs.title: Unit 9.5   Calls for Reform and Responses After 1900
1. An Era of Rights: Global Standards
docs.text:
In the wake of World War II, the international community sought to establish universal standards for human dignity.

  • Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948):

    • Established by the United Nations.

    • Asserted basic rights and fundamental freedoms for all humans regardless of race, gender, religion, or nationality.

    • The UN investigates abuses such as genocide, war crimes, and government oppression.

docs.title: 2. United Nations Specialized Agencies
docs.text:
• UNICEF (UN International Children's Emergency Fund):

    • Originally created to provide food for children after WWII.

    • Today: The world's largest provider of vaccines; provides education, health, and nutrition services for children globally.

  • UNHCR (UN Refugee Agency):

    • Provides food, medicine, and shelter to refugees (e.g., assisted refugees during the 1948 UN partition of Palestine).

  • International Court of Justice (World Court):

    • Settles legal disputes between countries (e.g., border disputes and treaty violations).

docs.title: 3. Global Feminism and Women's Rights
docs.text:
The 20th century saw a massive shift in the social and political status of women.

  • Suffrage: Women gained the right to vote in most parts of the world (e.g., US in 1920, UK in 1918/1928, Brazil in 1932).

  • Education: Literacy rates for women rose globally, leading to increased participation in the workforce.

  • CEDAW (1979): The Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women—often described as an international bill of rights for women.

docs.title: 4. Racial Equality and Anti-Apartheid Movements
docs.text:
• Apartheid in South Africa: A system of institutionalized racial segregation and discrimination.

  • Nelson Mandela: Leader of the African National Congress (ANC). Though imprisoned for 27 years, his leadership and international pressure (boycotts/sanctions) eventually led to the end of Apartheid.

  • 1994 Election: Mandela became the first Black president of South Africa in the nation's first fully representative democratic election.

  • Caste Discrimination: In India, the 1950 Constitution officially banned discrimination against Dalits (formerly "untouchables"), though social challenges remain.

docs.title: 5. Environmental and Economic Reform
docs.text:
• Green Belt Movement (1977):

    • Founded by Wangari Maathai in Kenya.

    • Empowered women to plant trees to combat deforestation and soil erosion.

    • Linked environmental conservation with women’s rights and political accountability.

  • Greenpeace: A multinational agency (now in 55+ countries) that advocates for the prevention of environmental destruction.

  • Fair Trade:

    • The World Fair Trade Organization (WFTO) was formed to prioritize people's rights over profits.

    • Focused on: Abolishing child labor, ensuring fair wages for farmers, and promoting sustainable farming.

docs.title: 6. Post-Communist Transitions
docs.text:
• End of the Cold War: As the USSR dissolved into 15 independent nations, many states transitioned from Command Economies (government-controlled) to Market Economies (private-sector-driven).

  • Protests: These transitions were often accompanied by social unrest and protests as people pushed for more democratic and transparent policies.

docs.title: 1. Global Brands and E-Commerce
docs.text:
The rise of the internet and the end of the Cold War allowed corporations to reach consumers in every corner of the globe.

  • Global Brands: Companies like Apple, Google, and Coca-Cola gained massive popularity through global distribution and aggressive advertising.

  • E-Commerce: Online shopping platforms revolutionized commerce by offering massive selections and instant transactions.

    • Amazon: Based in the US, dominates Western markets.

    • Alibaba: Based in China, represents the massive growth of the Asian consumer market.

  • Economic Regulation: Despite their global reach, these companies still pay various sales taxes based on the specific countries where they operate.

docs.title: 2. Social Media and Streaming
docs.text:
Technology changed not only how we buy things, but how we communicate and consume entertainment.

  • Instant Communication: Social media platforms made communication global and instantaneous, influencing public opinion and political discussions.

  • Streaming Video: Platforms like YouTube and Netflix allow cultural content (movies, music, documentaries) to cross borders instantly.

  • Government Censorship: Some nations restrict global platforms to control foreign influence.

    • China: Foreign social media (like Facebook or X) is banned.

    • Domestic Alternatives: China uses its own apps like WeChat, Weibo, and YuKu, which are monitored and censored by the government to prevent criticism of the Communist Party.

docs.title: 3. Globalization of Popular Culture
docs.text:
Culture became "hybridized" as traditions from one part of the world influenced another.

  • Music:

    • K-Pop: Originating in South Korea, it became a global phenomenon, illustrating how non-Western culture can dominate global charts.

    • Reggae: Originating in Jamaica, spreading messages of social justice and Rastafarianism worldwide.

  • Movies/TV: "Bollywood" (India) and the globalization of Hollywood films created a shared global visual language.

  • Sports: Events like the World Cup and the Olympics became massive global spectacles that foster a sense of "global community" while maintaining national rivalries.

docs.title: 4. New and Adapted Belief Systems
docs.text:
Globalization enabled the widespread promotion and reinterpretation of religious ideas.

  • Modernization of Religion: Many older religions saw their scriptures reinterpreted or practices modernized to fit a 20th-century lifestyle. There was a shift from "dogmatic collectivism" to more individualistic spiritual properties.

  • The New Age Movement:

    • A hybrid of Western culture and Eastern traditions (e.g., forms of Buddhism, Sufism, and Shamanism).

    • Focuses on an eclectic mix of old religious, occult, and mystical practices.

    • Emphasizes ideas like "Zen," "Channeling," and "Positivity."

  • Global Spread of Traditional Religions: Increased migration led to the growth of Islam in Europe, Christianity in Asia/Africa, and Hinduism in North America.

docs.title: 5. Global Consumer Culture
docs.text:
The 20th century saw the rise of a "consumerist" lifestyle where identity is often tied to the products one buys.

  • Fast Fashion: Companies like Zara or H&M produce clothing rapidly and cheaply to meet global demand, though this has led to criticism regarding environmental impact and labor conditions.

  • Fast Food: The "McDonaldization" of the world refers to the spread of standardized, efficient service models across different cultures.

docs.title: 6. Challenges to Cultural Globalization
docs.text:
• Cultural Imperialism: The fear that Western (specifically American) culture would overwhelm and erase local traditions.

  • Resistance: Some groups emphasize "localism" or "traditionalism" to protect their unique cultural heritage from being homogenized by global brands.

docs.title: 1. Overview of Resistance
docs.text: While globalization brought economic growth and cultural exchange, it also faced significant pushback. Resistance stems from concerns over economic exploitation, loss of national sovereignty, and the erosion of local cultures.

docs.title: 2. Economic Resistance
docs.text:
Critics argue that global economic structures benefit wealthy nations and corporations at the expense of the poor and the environment.

  • Anti-IMF and Anti-World Bank Activism:

    • Protesters claim these institutions prioritize debt repayment over social services (like education and healthcare) in developing nations.

    • Activists often target meetings of the World Trade Organization (WTO) to protest labor exploitation and environmental damage.

  • Labor Concerns:

    • Child Labor: Globalization often leads to the use of child labor in developing nations to keep production costs low.

    • Rana Plaza Disaster: The 2013 collapse of a garment factory in Bangladesh highlighted the dangerous working conditions in the global "fast fashion" supply chain.

    • Amazon: Resistance against large corporations due to reports of poor working conditions and the displacement of local small businesses.

docs.title: 3. Political Resistance and Sovereignty
docs.text:
Some nations and movements resist globalization to protect their political independence and national identity.

  • Brexit (The British Exit):

    • In 2016, 52% of British voters chose to leave the European Union (EU).

    • Reasons: Belief that the EU was no longer delivering economic prosperity, concerns over immigration/border control, and a desire to regain "sovereignty" from EU regulations.

    • Outcome: After years of negotiation (under Prime Ministers like Theresa May), the UK officially left the EU in 2020.

  • North Korea:

    • Perhaps the most extreme example of anti-globalization.

    • The government strictly limits foreign influence (media, internet, travel) to maintain the current regime and prevent cultural "contamination."

docs.title: 4. Cultural and Social Resistance
docs.text:
The fear of "Cultural Imperialism" (the dominance of one culture over others) has led to various forms of pushback.

  • Local Social Media: To counter the influence of Western platforms and maintain state control, countries like China have developed their own versions, such as Weibo and WeChat.

  • Religious and Traditional Movements: Some groups view globalization as a threat to traditional moral values and religious practices, leading to a rise in fundamentalist movements that reject "Westernization."

docs.title: 5. Environmental Resistance
docs.text:
The pursuit of global industrial growth is often seen as a direct threat to the planet.

  • Ecotourism: As a response to destructive mass tourism, ecotourism focuses on responsible travel to natural areas that conserves the environment and improves the well-being of local people.

  • Global Climate Strikes: Led by figures like Greta Thunberg, these movements resist the corporate-led "business as usual" approach to the environment, demanding that governments prioritize the planet over global trade profits.

docs.title: AP World History Unit 9.1: Advances in Technology and Exchange
1. The "Shrinking World" Concept
docs.text:
• Definition: The idea that while the physical size of the Earth remains constant, the time it takes to travel and communicate across it has decreased so drastically that it "feels" smaller.

  • Communication Technology:

    • Early 1900s: Letters took weeks via ship.

    • Mid-20th Century: Radio and television spread news in real-time.

    • Late 20th/21st Century: The Internet and mobile phones created instant, global connectivity.

  • Transportation Technology:

    • Shipping Containers: Standardized metal boxes that made it incredibly cheap and efficient to move goods globally, fueling the rise of global supply chains.

    • Commercial Air Travel: Reduced travel time between continents from weeks (by ship) to hours.

docs.title: 2. Energy Revolutions
docs.text:
• Fossil Fuels: Continued reliance on oil and coal powered the massive industrial growth of the 20th century.

  • Nuclear Power: Emerged as a potent but controversial alternative for electricity after WWII.

  • Renewables: Recent shifts toward solar and wind power in response to climate change.

docs.title: 3. The Green Revolution (Mid-20th Century)
docs.text:
• Goal: To increase food production and fight global hunger, particularly in developing nations.

  • Methods: Development of high-yield seeds (GMOs), increased use of chemical fertilizers, and advanced irrigation.

  • Impact:

    • Positive: Massive increase in world population and food security.

    • Negative: Environmental damage from pesticides, loss of genetic diversity in crops, and small farmers being pushed out by large agribusinesses.

docs.title: AP World History Unit 9.8 & 9.9: Global Institutions and Continuity/Change
1. Political Institutions: The United Nations (UN)
docs.text:
• Purpose: Founded in 1945 to replace the failed League of Nations. Aims to maintain international peace and security.

  • Key Components:

    • Security Council: Can authorize military action (5 permanent members: US, UK, France, Russia, China).

    • General Assembly: Discussion body for all member nations.

docs.title: 2. Economic Institutions: The Bretton Woods System
docs.text:
• IMF (International Monetary Fund): Provides short-term loans to prevent currency collapse.

  • World Bank: Provides long-term loans for development projects in "Global South" nations.

  • WTO (World Trade Organization): Works to reduce tariffs and trade barriers (replaced GATT).

docs.title: 3. Global Security and Conflict
docs.text:
• Post-Cold War Challenges: Rise of international terrorism (e.g., Al-Qaeda and the 9/11 attacks) led to new global security protocols and the "War on Terror."

  • Regional Blocs: The European Union (EU) created a single market and currency (the Euro) to integrate Europe and prevent another world war.

docs.title: 4. Continuity and Change (Unit 9.9)
docs.text:
• Continuity: Humans still rely on trade, social hierarchies remain (though they are being challenged), and regional identities persist despite global culture.

  • Change: The speed of interaction is unprecedented; the scale of environmental impact is now global rather than local; and non-state actors (MNCs, NGOs) now hold power traditionally reserved for governments.

docs.title: LaTeX Math Support
docs.text:
LaTeX math is supported using MathJax.

Inline math looks like \(f(x) = x^2\). The input for this is $f(x) = x^2$. Use $...$.

For a block of math, use $$...$$ on separate lines

$$
F(x) = \int^a_b \frac{1}{2}x^4
$$

gives

\[ F(x) = \int^a_b \frac{1}{2}x^4 \]
